retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Evaluation -- Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: A critical step in this list is consent; some patients and caregivers may find it difficult to address the topic of a stillborn autopsy. Discussing the autopsy with clear communication while respecting the parent or caregiver's values and addressing their concerns. Parents/caregivers may also request a limited autopsy (on a specific organ/system), external examination only, noninvasive autopsy, or minimally invasive autopsy is important. Additionally, fetal tissue is not considered part of the product of conception after 20 weeks of gestational age, and therefore, insurance may not cover its examination.

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Autopsy -- Issues of Concern -- Aims and Objectives of a Forensic Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: However, it is noteworthy that the final verdict regarding the manner of death is decided by the Court of Law in most jurisdictions worldwide since opinion on the manner of death is based not just on medical evidence but more so on other circumstantial pieces of evidence. Medical evidence is only one piece of the puzzle. In the case of a fetal autopsy or an autopsy of a neonate, the following are the specific medicolegal objectives: To determine the intra-uterine/gestational age of the fetus, ultimately aiming to know whether the fetus was viable or not (to assess the viability of the fetus) If viable, to determine whether it was born alive (live birth) or dead (dead birth/stillbirth) If born alive, to determine the period of survival after birth, the cause, and manner of death

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Evaluation -- Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: A fetal autopsy is a detailed examination of the external and internal structures of the fetal body to identify any congenital anomalies, infections, or other pathological conditions. [93] An autopsy is useful in establishing a cause of death in 42.4% (95% CI: 36.9–48.4) of cases. [92] A comprehensive and detailed documentation of both positive and negative findings is crucial. Complete fetal autopsy components include:

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: SIDS is a diagnosis of exclusion requiring a thorough investigation, including the review of the clinical history, death scene investigation, and a complete autopsy. Recommendations include detailed interviews with caregivers, a review of medical records, reports of the death scene observations, and a complete autopsy within 24 hours of death. Existing internationally standardized protocols for autopsy and national guidelines for the death scene investigation are effective tools to assist with the completion of the investigation. The CDC has established SUID monitoring programs in 22 states and jurisdiction, but no national protocol is available in the United States as state laws govern medicolegal death investigation.

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Evaluation -- Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: Consent External gross examination: Photographs, radiographs, body measurements, document maceration, overall appearance, skin sample for cytogenetics Internal gross examination: Y-incision, routine cultures, in situ examination of abdomen/neck/thorax Removal of the organ block and brain, dissection of individual organs (all organs weighed and any abnormalities recorded) Brain gross and histological examination after fixation Histologic examination of tissues Review and recording of gross and histological findings Provisional anatomic diagnosis Review of all ancillary studies (genetic, metabolic, microbiology) Report including final anatomic diagnosis, summary of clinical history, gross description, microscopic description, and clinical pathologic correlation [93]

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Evaluation -- Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: An autopsy may identify congenital abnormalities, injuries, infection, or metabolic defects. Autopsy identifies the cause of death in only 15% of suspected SIDS deaths. [28] The autopsy includes external and internal examination, radiologic evaluation, microbiology, toxicology, and laboratory studies. Essential laboratory evaluation incudes: electrolytes, screening for inborn errors of metabolism, and screening for genetic disorders/polymorphisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Death Certification -- Other Issues -- Brain Death in Children
retrieved_docs.content: Brain death determination in pediatrics is a clinical diagnosis. There is not enough data on brain death on an infant less than 37 weeks of age. Therefore, the recommendations listed below do not apply to infants less than 37 weeks of age.

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists, the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (RCOG), the Perinatal Society of Australia and New Zealand, and the Society of Obstetricians and Gynecologists of Canada summarized guidelines for stillbirth evaluation in 2020 (see Table. The 2020 Guidelines for Stillbirth Investigation). [90] Stillbirth definitions vary slightly among organizations. While all organizations recommend investigations, the extent of testing varies. Notably, RCOG uniquely emphasizes obtaining a second-opinion ultrasound for confirmation. The placental examination is universally recommended if an autopsy is declined, as it can provide valuable insights into the cause of stillbirth. Although an autopsy is the preferred method for determining cause, it requires parental consent, and imaging, such as magnetic resonance imaging, is considered a viable alternative when an autopsy is not performed. [De Gruyter. Investigation and management of stillbirth: a descriptive review of major guidelines. https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/jpm-2021-0403/html?lang=en ]

retrieved_docs.title: Forensic Autopsy -- Issues of Concern -- Aims and Objectives of a Forensic Autopsy
retrieved_docs.content: The aims and objectives of a forensic autopsy differ from one case to another and can be specific for a particular case. Nevertheless, in general, the following are the objectives of conducting a forensic autopsy: To establish the identity of the dead To determine the cause of death To assist in confirming or refuting the alleged manner of death, wherever possible To estimate the time since death (postmortem interval) Establishing the identity of the deceased is of paramount importance while conducting a forensic autopsy on an unknown body. At times, confirming the identity of the deceased also matters.

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Micro-computed tomography has emerged as a valuable noninvasive tool for diagnosing stillbirth, particularly in early gestation fetuses, by providing high-resolution 3-dimensional imaging of small anatomical structures. Study results have demonstrated its high diagnostic accuracy, with a sensitivity of 93.8% (95% CI: 71.7%–98.9%) and a specificity of 100% (95% CI: 82.4%–100%), making it a reliable alternative to traditional autopsy for parents who decline invasive postmortem examinations. [105] Similarly, postmortem MRI has demonstrated diagnostic accuracy ranging from 77% to 94% for pregnancies beyond 20 weeks. Higher-field MRI scanners, such as the 9.4 Tesla, hold promise for improving diagnostic capabilities in earlier gestations. Additionally, postmortem ultrasound using high-frequency probes offers a more accessible and cost-effective alternative, with sensitivity and specificity ranging from 67% to 77% and 74% to 90%, respectively. These emerging imaging modalities provide valuable insights into stillbirth diagnosis while offering noninvasive options that are both parent-friendly and clinically informative. [96]

retrieved_docs.title: Death Certification -- Other Issues -- Observation Period
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 examinations are separated by an observation period of: Twenty-four hours; for newborn up to 30 days Twelve hours for infants and children older than 30 days to 18 years [18] The first examination determines if the child has met all the required criteria for brain death. The second examination is to confirm brain death based on the fact that the condition is irreversible.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Bone Ossification -- Clinical Significance -- Forensic Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Age estimation of the fetus is one of the primary objectives of the fetal autopsy. Forensic fetal osteology: The embryological method is one of the procedures employed in estimating the gestational age of the fetus, which is crucial in determining fetal viability postpartum in forensic practice. The forensic examination of fetal remains [19] [20] [21] [22] :

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging studies such as postmortem MRI, ultrasound, and computed tomography (CT) scans serve as valuable tools for stillbirth evaluation, particularly when autopsy is declined. Postmortem MRI, the most studied method, provides high diagnostic accuracy (77%–94%), especially for fetuses beyond 20 weeks' gestation. In contrast, microfocus CT is highly effective for those with gestation of less than 20 weeks, achieving near-total agreement with autopsy findings. These imaging techniques, used alongside placental pathology and genetic testing, offer a noninvasive means of obtaining critical diagnostic information to better understand stillbirth causes. [96]

retrieved_docs.title: Stillbirth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, stillbirth is generally defined as fetal death occurring at a minimum birth weight of 350 grams or a minimum gestational age of 20 weeks, with reporting requirements enforced at these thresholds. The WHO, however, defines stillbirth as fetal death at a minimum of 28 weeks' gestation. This distinction, along with some variation among states, may impact national and international comparisons of stillbirth rates. Standardization of the definition of stillbirth is a current priority. [1] "Stillbirth" has replaced "intrauterine fetal demise" as the terminology of choice based on the opinions of parent groups.

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS) is the abrupt and unexplained death of an infant less than 1-year old. Despite investigation (review of clinical history, investigation of the death, and a complete autopsy), no evidence supports a specific single cause of death. [1] SIDS frequently occurs during sleep, and it is the leading cause of death in infants one to twelve months of age in the United States. Due to inconsistencies in the use of SIDS as a diagnostic term, sudden unexpected infant death (SUID) was introduced. SUID includes SIDS, accidental suffocation/asphyxia, and deaths due to uncertain circumstances.

retrieved_docs.title: Grief Support Following In-Hospital Deaths -- Other Issues -- Special Situations
retrieved_docs.content: Autopsy and medical examiner cases: Depending on state laws, deaths meeting established criteria must be promptly reported to the medical examiner or coroner. Some examples are unexplained or unusual deaths, homicides or suicides, medical procedure deaths, pediatric deaths, accidents, or unnatural deaths. If the death qualifies as a medical examiner or coroner case, resuscitative lines and tubes must not be removed from the deceased. If the death does not fall under medical examiner or coroner jurisdiction, the physician is still obligated to offer the family the option of an autopsy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 141
retrieved_docs.content: lesions, fetal demise, and undefined obstetric indications within a comprehensive framework that guides clinicians in assessing how each factor may affect maternal and fetal outcomes and informs comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2869
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery finding is a clinical observation that a baby is coming out during the birthing process, and it is classified as a ولادة جنين ميت (dead‑fetus delivery). This indicates that while the delivery finding describes the act of the infant emerging, the specific type of delivery identified is a stillbirth. Recognizing this distinction is important for accurate documentation, appropriate obstetric management, and counseling of the patient and family.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2060
retrieved_docs.content: Delivery finding is a clinical observation that can be classified as a type (isa) of “baby is coming out,” indicating a live birth, and also as a type (isa) of “ولادة جنين ميت” (dead‑fetus delivery). The condition “إجهاض فائت” (missed abortion) has a relationship with “ولادة جنين ميت,” reflecting that a missed abortion may be associated with or result in a dead‑fetus delivery. This integrated view highlights the spectrum of delivery outcomes—from live birth to non‑viable fetal delivery—and underscores the importance of accurately distinguishing these scenarios for appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Duration of gestation refers to the length of time a fetus remains in the uterus before birth, and the classification of a birth as liveborn is encompassed within this gestational timeframe; therefore, liveborn status is defined by the specific duration of gestation at delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1799
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) and forceps delivery (الولادة بالملقط) are clinically associated obstetric interventions. Both procedures are employed within the spectrum of pregnancy management: elective termination represents a planned ending of gestation, while forceps delivery is a method of assisted vaginal birth used when specific maternal or fetal conditions warrant intervention. Their clinical association highlights the range of obstetric care options available to address diverse maternal‑fetal indications, and recognizing this relationship assists clinicians in selecting the most appropriate intervention to optimize outcomes for both mother and newborn.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 232
retrieved_docs.content: with attention to contraindications, and how structured terminologies provide organized frameworks for perinatal and pediatric diagnoses, supporting comprehensive patient assessment and care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2407
retrieved_docs.content: إجهاض فائت (missed abortion) is linked to ولادة جنين ميت (birth of a dead fetus), indicating that a missed abortion can be associated with the delivery of a non‑viable fetus. Moreover, ولادة جنين ميت is a member of the Standardized MedDRA Query (SMQ) titled إنهاء الحمل وخطر الإجهاض (Termination of pregnancy and risk of abortion), which groups together adverse events related to pregnancy termination and fetal loss. These connections illustrate how missed abortion and stillbirth are interrelated concepts within broader safety‑monitoring frameworks for adverse pregnancy outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 753
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal structures (plodové struktury) are described as having a finding site that is composite and located (samengesteld; ligging). These same fetal structures are also associated with a missed abortion (إجهاض فائت). A missed abortion (إجهاض فائت) is further related to the outcome of a stillbirth of a dead fetus (ولادة جنين ميت).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1497
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA Center for Devices and Radiological Health Terminology includes the reproductive health concepts إجهاض فائت (missed abortion) and العقم (infertility) within its defined subset of terms. In this framework, إجهاض فائت is linked to ولادة جنين ميت (birth of a dead fetus), reflecting the clinical relationship between a fetal death that remains in utero and the eventual delivery of a non‑viable infant. Recognizing both إجهاض فائت and العقم in the FDA terminology underscores their importance for device regulation, diagnostic classification, and the management of patients experiencing these reproductive disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.
